nativeRange.habitat: Open pelagic mid-water zones along rocky shorelines and in offshore areas of Lake Tanganyika; forms large mixed-sex schools in the water column, typically 3–10 m depth but ranging deeper

decorPreferences: large open mid-water swimming space (essential for schooling behaviour)
rocky shoreline or boulder cluster at tank margins for perimeter structure
minimal substrate clutter in the centre
low or no planting (mimics barren pelagic biotope)
tight-fitting lid (moderate jumping risk when startled)

diet.notes: In the wild feeds primarily on zooplankton, copepods, and small crustaceans suspended in the open water column. In the aquarium accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, baby brine shrimp (live or frozen), cyclops, daphnia, and finely chopped mysis shrimp. Avoid exclusively large foods — small particle sizes suit the relatively small mouth. Feed small portions 2–3 times daily; constant-grazer tendencies mean single large feedings are less effective than multiple small ones. Vitamin-enriched frozen foods help maintain colour in the many captive colour forms.

goodWith: shell-dwelling cichlids (Neolamprologus multifasciatus, N. ocellatus) on the tank floor
Altolamprologus calvus and A. compressiceps (rock-dwelling, non-competitive mid-water)
Synodontis multipunctatus (fw-fish-170) — natural brood parasite but compatible tank companion at appropriate stocking levels
avoidWith: large aggressive cichlids (Cyphotilapia frontosa, large Tropheus)
fast-swimming piscivores that predate small cichlids
unrelated African rift-lake cichlids with different water-chemistry needs (Malawi/Victoria species best avoided in biotope tanks)
very small fish that may be inadvertently consumed

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image; check current availability. Shows typical male blue-and-yellow colouration.
Aquarium photograph showing schooling behaviour; verify current file existence on Commons.

summary: A slender, open-water schooling cichlid from Lake Tanganyika — unusual among rift-lake cichlids in being fully pelagic. Maternal mouthbrooder that releases free-swimming fry in mid-water. Available in many regional colour forms; intermediate care due to strict Tanganyikan water-chemistry requirements.
careNotes: Cyprichromis leptosoma is one of the very few cichlids adapted to the open pelagic zone of Lake Tanganyika, schooling in large groups far from the rocky substrate that characterises most Tanganyikan biotopes. This mid-water lifestyle is reflected in tank requirements: ample open swimming space is non-negotiable, and the recommended minimum footprint of 120 cm (200 L) accommodates a proper school of eight or more fish. Tanganyikan water chemistry is essential — maintain pH 7.8–9.0, moderate to high hardness (gH 10–20, kH 8–18), and temperature 24–27 °C. Regular partial water changes using a dechlorinated, mineral-buffered source help prevent pH swings; crushed coral or aragonite substrate and/or commercial Tanganyika salt mixes are widely used to maintain buffering capacity. The species is available in numerous regional colour forms (e.g., “Jumbo Yellow,” “Karilani Tricolor,” “Tembwe Blue,” “Chituta”) which differ in male nuptial colouration; mixing geographic forms is generally discouraged by enthusiasts who prefer biotope authenticity, though hybrids are reproductively viable. Males are strikingly coloured with yellow, blue, or bi-coloured fins depending on the locality form; females are drabber silver-grey. Conspecific aggression is low and better managed by keeping a larger group — the aggression is diluted across more fish. A tight-fitting lid is advisable as the species can jump when startled. Do not house with boisterous or larger cichlids that will out-compete it at feeding or harass the school.
breedingNotes: Cyprichromis leptosoma is a maternal mouthbrooder with the unusual distinction of releasing free-swimming fry directly into open water rather than at the substrate — paralleling its pelagic lifestyle. Spawning occurs in mid-water: the female picks up eggs immediately after fertilisation and broods them for approximately 21–28 days. The male's anal-fin egg spots (ocelli) serve as a spawning stimulus in the classic "T-position" spawning embrace. Broods are small, typically 5–20 fry. The female should not be removed to a breeding tank unless absolutely necessary, as stress commonly induces premature release or swallowing of the brood. Post-release fry are large enough to accept newly hatched brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii) and finely powdered fry foods immediately. Cuckoo Catfish (Synodontis multipunctatus, fw-fish-170) is a documented brood parasite of Cyprichromis in the wild: the catfish times its own egg-release to coincide with the cichlid spawning event, and the mouthbrooding female inadvertently incubates catfish eggs alongside her own. Aquarists keeping S. multipunctatus with C. leptosoma should be aware that parasite-triggered brood failure is possible, though many keepers report successful co-habitation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Cyprichromis leptosoma
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters, biotope, breeding behaviour (maternal mouthbrooder, mid-water fry release), minimum tank dimensions, diet, and colour-form notes. Confirmed pelagic schooling lifestyle and cuckoo catfish brood-parasitism behaviour.

additional.name: FishBase — Cyprichromis leptosoma
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (to 13 cm TL), native range (Lake Tanganyika endemic), depth range, temperature tolerance, and diet (zooplankton / copepods). Confirmed endemic status restricted to Lake Tanganyika.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Lake Tanganyika Cichlids Care Guide
additional.notes: General Tanganyika cichlid husbandry guide used to cross-check water parameter recommendations (pH 7.8–9.0, temperature 76–82°F / 24–28°C) and stocking approaches. Noted Cyprichromis as open-water schooling species suitable for community Tanganyika setups.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cyprichromis leptosoma species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for colour-form diversity (regional variants including Jumbo Yellow, Karilani, Tembwe), breeding difficulty, group-size recommendations (minimum 8–10 fish), and notes on cuckoo catfish brood parasitism. Confirmed intermediate care level.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum.com — Cyprichromis leptosoma species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community source. Provided additional confirmation of maternal mouthbrooding mechanics, mid-water spawning behaviour, brood size (5–20 fry), incubation period (~3–4 weeks), and co-habitation notes with Synodontis multipunctatus.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; spawns in mid-water and releases free-swimming fry directly into open water (unusual among cichlids). Broods are small (5–20 fry); females should not be disturbed during the 21–28-day incubation period. Cuckoo Catfish (Synodontis multipunctatus) is a documented brood parasite and may cause brood failure if housed together.
